refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and operational areas.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate captured spatial features into a unified worksite model accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The parent FR (CR-NGHE-006) remains a coordination node; each sub-FR is atomic and solution-neutral, enabling independent DP selection and IM assignment.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query API.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel grid with occupancy and semantic labels; update grid incrementally as new observations arrive; convert grid to feature primitives (planes, edges, clusters) on demand for planning; expose both raw grid and derived features.

DP4: Ingest raw sensor streams and apply real-time feature extraction (edge detection, plane fitting, object classification) to produce a feature event stream; aggregate events into a temporal feature database; provide time-windowed and spatial queries.

DP5: Define a feature schema (geometry, attributes, safety tags) aligned with worksite safety plan and operational policies; use schema-driven ingestion pipeline to validate and normalize incoming observations; store in a schema-compliant repository; enforce schema constraints at query time.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5 (with DP1 as complementary realization): Define a feature schema (geometry, attributes, safety tags) aligned with worksite safety plan and operational policies; use schema-driven ingestion pipeline to validate and normalize incoming observations; store in a schema-compliant repository; enforce schema constraints at query time. Combine with multi-modal sensing fusion (DP1) to acquire and normalize raw spatial data into schema-compliant feature records.

potential_sources: Schema coupling: If feature schema is tightly bound to a specific sensor modality (e.g., assumes LiDAR point density), it will couple to sensor choice and reduce flexibility for alternative acquisition methods.

Policy coupling: If schema encodes specific safety rules (e.g., 'minimum clearance = 2m') rather than tagging features with policy-relevant attributes, changes to operational policies will require schema redesign.
Downstream coupling: If query interface exposes internal schema structure directly, planning/execution functions become brittle to schema evolution.
Temporal coupling: If schema does not account for feature observation timestamps and confidence, temporal reasoning and multi-observation fusion become difficult.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 + DP1 achieves good separation of concerns: schema definition (policy/requirement-driven) is decoupled from sensing modality (DP1 handles fusion). Risk is in schema design itself—must remain abstract enough to accommodate multiple sensor types and policy variants, while concrete enough to guide ingestion and validation. Recommend schema versioning and a stable query abstraction layer to isolate downstream consumers from schema changes.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable or change infrequently enough to allow schema-driven design. RISK: If policies are highly dynamic or context-dependent, schema-driven approach may become a bottleneck; mitigation: design schema with policy-attribute tagging rather than hard-coded rules.

ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensor fusion is feasible and produces sufficient accuracy for feature extraction. RISK: Sensor calibration, synchronization, and environmental noise may degrade feature quality; mitigation: include confidence/uncertainty metadata in schema; design ingestion pipeline with outlier detection.

ASSUMPTION: Feature set required by planning/execution is known and bounded. RISK: Unforeseen feature types or attributes may emerge during operation; mitigation: design schema with extensibility (e.g., key-value attributes, versioning) and establish change-control process.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite geometry is sufficiently static during a planning/execution cycle. RISK: Dynamic obstacles, temporary structures, or personnel movement may invalidate model; mitigation: include observation timestamp and validity window in schema; support incremental updates.

ASSUMPTION: Query interface abstraction is stable. RISK: If planning/execution functions require frequent query pattern changes, interface becomes a coupling point; mitigation: design query API at a logical level (e.g., 'features within region with safety tag X') rather than low-level schema navigation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required feature types and attributes (e.g., obstacles, hazards, access points, surface properties, safety zones) aligned with worksite safety plan; (2) Acceptable latency and update frequency for model availability; (3) Confidence/uncertainty requirements for feature geometry and classification; (4) Expected worksite scale and feature density; (5) Extensibility requirements (can new feature types be added without redesign?). This will enable schema design that is both policy-aligned and resilient to change. Additionally, establish a clear contract between the spatial model (DP5 + DP1) and planning/execution consumers: define a stable query abstraction (e.g., 'retrieve features of type T within region R with safety constraints C') that decouples model internals from consumer logic.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for defining, validating, and enforcing safety feature schemas; managing schema-compliant repositories; and enforcing schema constraints at query time. Acts as the authoritative source for safety observation normalization and constraint enforcement across the system.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw multi-modal sensor observations (spatial, temporal, categorical) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; feeds into schema-driven ingestion pipeline for validation and normalization.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits constraint violations, schema validation failures, and derived safety alerts based on schema-compliant feature records and query-time enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational policy updates and worksite safety plan definitions that inform schema evolution; returns schema-compliant feature queries and constraint status to task planners.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of schema definition, validation pipelines, constraint enforcement, and safety-compliant repositories. Schema-driven ingestion and query-time enforcement are core safety functions. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry ingestion (in), SafetyAlert emission (out), and Tasking coordination (inout) cross boundaries. Multi-modal fusion (DP1) integration point is well-defined via Telemetry interface.

Poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level orchestrator/integration layer, not a domain-specific safety module. Placing schema definition and constraint enforcement here would scatter safety logic across the system and blur NGHE's role as a coordinator. Schema validation and repository management would diffuse into Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, violating separation of concerns.

Very poor fit. Vehicle is a hardware/sensor platform, not a data governance or safety logic layer. Embedding schema definition, validation pipelines, and constraint enforcement in Vehicle would couple safety policy to hardware, making schema evolution difficult and forcing Vehicle to manage system-wide safety repositories. High diffusion: schema logic would need to propagate to Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a data governance layer. While Teleoperation may consume schema-compliant features for operator awareness, it should not own schema definition, validation, or constraint enforcement. Placing these responsibilities here would couple operator control logic to safety policy and create high diffusion to Vehicle and Safety Services.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the single authoritative owner of the feature schema, schema-driven ingestion pipeline, schema-compliant repository, and query-time constraint enforcement. This module receives raw observations via Telemetry (from Vehicle and Teleoperation), validates and normalizes them against the schema, stores compliant records, and enforces constraints at query time. Operational policies and worksite safety plans flow in via Tasking to inform schema evolution. Safety violations and alerts flow out via SafetyAlert. This design achieves high cohesion (all schema governance is co-located) and low coupling (only three well-defined interface channels cross the boundary).
containment_risks: Risk: If multi-modal fusion (DP1) is implemented as a distributed algorithm across Vehicle and Teleoperation, the schema-driven ingestion pipeline in Safety Services may receive heterogeneous or partially-fused observations, requiring Safety Services to handle fusion ambiguity. Mitigation: Ensure DP1 (multi-modal fusion) is also owned by Safety Services or a tightly-coupled sub-module, so that raw sensor data is fused into a canonical form before schema validation.

Risk: If operational policies and worksite safety plans are defined outside Safety Services (e.g., in NGHE Solution or a separate policy engine), schema evolution may lag policy changes, creating a consistency gap. Mitigation: Establish a clear Tasking interface contract that ensures Safety Services is notified of policy changes and has authority to update schema definitions in response.

Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems cache or replicate schema-compliant features locally for performance, constraint enforcement at query time in Safety Services may not catch violations in cached data. Mitigation: Enforce a single source of truth for schema-compliant repositories in Safety Services; Vehicle and Teleoperation may cache read-only views but must query Safety Services for authoritative constraint checks.

Risk: High throughput of raw Telemetry observations may create a bottleneck in Safety Services' ingestion pipeline if schema validation is synchronous. Mitigation: Design the ingestion pipeline with asynchronous validation and buffering; use SafetyAlert to communicate validation failures back to sources without blocking observation ingestion.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP5 is necessary. The selected DP is well-aligned with Safety Services as the implementation module. However, to strengthen containment and reduce diffusion, explicitly clarify that DP1 (multi-modal sensing fusion) should be co-located with DP5 in Safety Services, or at minimum, that the fusion output is a canonical, schema-compliant feature record that is the sole input to the schema-driven ingestion pipeline. This ensures that schema validation operates on a consistent, normalized input format and avoids diffusion of fusion logic across Vehicle and Teleoperation. If DP1 must remain distributed (e.g., for latency or hardware constraints), define a strict interface contract (via Telemetry) that specifies the fused observation format and validation rules that Vehicle and Teleoperation must respect before emitting observations to Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (schema-driven feature definition, validation, normalization, and constraint enforcement) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (capture and represent worksite spatial features in machine-readable model). The DP is concrete, testable, and includes explicit mechanisms (schema definition, ingestion pipeline, repository, query-time enforcement) that map to the functional requirement. DP1 (multi-modal fusion) is a complementary realization that feeds normalized observations into the schema pipeline, closing the acquisition-to-representation chain. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: DP1 (multi-modal fusion) diffusion risk: If fusion logic remains distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems rather than being co-located or tightly coupled with DP5 in Safety Services, the schema-driven ingestion pipeline may receive heterogeneous or partially-fused observations, requiring Safety Services to handle fusion ambiguity and increasing validation complexity. Mitigation: Establish DP1 ownership clarity; ensure fused observations conform to a strict canonical format before entering Safety Services.

Schema evolution lag risk: If operational policies and worksite safety plans are defined outside Safety Services (e.g., in NGHE Solution or external policy engine), schema updates may not propagate promptly, creating consistency gaps between policy intent and schema constraints. Mitigation: Enforce a Tasking interface contract that makes Safety Services the authoritative schema owner and ensures policy changes trigger schema validation and update workflows.

Cached replica consistency risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems cache or replicate schema-compliant features locally for performance, query-time constraint enforcement in Safety Services may not detect violations in stale cached data. Mitigation: Enforce single source of truth in Safety Services; permit read-only caching but require authoritative constraint checks via Safety Services queries.

Ingestion pipeline throughput bottleneck: High-frequency raw Telemetry observations may create synchronous validation bottlenecks in Safety Services if schema validation is blocking. Mitigation: Design asynchronous validation with buffering; use SafetyAlert to communicate failures without blocking observation ingestion.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services achieves excellent containment (0.92 score) for DP5. Schema definition, validation pipelines, constraint enforcement, and schema-compliant repositories are naturally co-located in a single authoritative module. External interfaces are minimal and well-defined: Telemetry (in), SafetyAlert (out), Tasking (inout). The module boundary is clear and separation of concerns is maintained. Alternative candidates (NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation) all exhibit poor containment (0.3–0.45) and high diffusion (0.55–0.7) due to role mismatch and coupling risks. Safety Services is the correct ownership choice. Recommended strengthening: explicitly clarify DP1 ownership and ensure fused observations are canonical before schema validation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criteria ('worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features') is directly verifiable via Safety Services' schema-compliant repository and query interface. Verification can be performed by: (1) confirming schema definition aligns with worksite safety plan and operational policies; (2) validating that raw Telemetry observations are successfully ingested, normalized, and stored; (3) querying the repository and confirming returned features match the agreed schema and satisfy constraint rules; (4) testing that SafetyAlert correctly flags schema violations and constraint breaches. Traceability from FR to DP to IM is clear and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Safety Services is unambiguously allocated as the implementation module for DP5. The module's scope, responsibilities (schema definition, validation, repository management, constraint enforcement), and interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking inout) are explicitly defined. Ownership is singular and non-overlapping with other subsystems. The only allocation ambiguity is DP1 (multi-modal fusion) ownership; recommended revision clarifies whether fusion is co-located in Safety Services or distributed with a strict interface contract. Once DP1 ownership is resolved, allocation clarity is complete.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly assign ownership of DP1 (multi-modal sensing fusion) to Safety Services or define a strict interface contract (via Telemetry) that specifies the canonical fused observation format, validation rules, and schema compliance requirements that Vehicle and Teleoperation must respect before emitting observations. This eliminates the primary diffusion risk and ensures schema validation operates on consistent, normalized input.

Establish a formal Tasking interface contract that designates Safety Services as the authoritative owner of feature schema definitions and ensures that operational policy updates and worksite safety plan changes trigger schema validation and evolution workflows in Safety Services. This mitigates schema evolution lag risk.

Document a single-source-of-truth policy for schema-compliant repositories: Safety Services owns the authoritative repository; Vehicle and Teleoperation may cache read-only views but must query Safety Services for constraint checks. This mitigates cached replica consistency risk.

Design the Safety Services ingestion pipeline with asynchronous validation and buffering to handle high-frequency Telemetry observations without blocking. Use SafetyAlert to communicate validation failures without synchronous blocking. This mitigates throughput bottleneck risk.

Add explicit acceptance criteria for DP1 integration: 'Fused observations from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems conform to a canonical schema-compliant format before ingestion into Safety Services; schema validation success rate is ≥99% for well-formed observations; validation latency does not exceed [agreed threshold] for [agreed percentile].' This makes DP1–DP5 integration verifiable.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the functional requirement is well-addressed by the design and implementation strategy. However, the gate results identify critical diffusion and ownership risks that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The primary issue is DP1 (multi-modal fusion) ownership ambiguity: if fusion logic remains distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems without a strict canonical interface contract, the schema-driven validation pipeline in Safety Services will receive heterogeneous observations, undermining the integrity of the worksite spatial model. Given high safety criticality, this ambiguity must be eliminated at the current level before decomposing further. Additionally, the Tasking interface contract and single-source-of-truth policy for schema repositories require explicit definition to prevent schema evolution lag and cached replica consistency failures. These are architectural decisions that belong at the current decomposition level, not sub-levels. Once revisions are incorporated, the FR-DP-IM chain will be sufficiently robust to support safe sub-FR decomposition.

revise.why: DP1 (multi-modal fusion) and DP5 (schema-driven feature definition and validation) must be explicitly integrated with clear ownership and interface contracts. Recommended revisions: (1) Assign DP1 ownership to Safety Services with explicit responsibility for fused observation canonicalization, or define a strict Telemetry interface contract specifying canonical format, validation rules, and schema compliance requirements that Vehicle and Teleoperation must enforce before emission. (2) Establish a formal Tasking interface contract designating Safety Services as authoritative schema owner and ensuring policy-to-schema update workflows. (3) Define a single-source-of-truth policy: Safety Services owns the authoritative schema-compliant repository; Vehicle and Teleoperation may cache read-only views but must query Safety Services for constraint enforcement. (4) Design Safety Services ingestion pipeline with asynchronous validation and buffering to prevent throughput bottlenecks. (5) Add explicit acceptance criteria for DP1–DP5 integration: fused observations conform to canonical schema-compliant format; schema validation success rate ≥99%; validation latency within agreed threshold. These revisions transform DP1 and DP5 from logically sound but architecturally ambiguous designs into a cohesive, verifiable, and safety-critical implementation strategy.

assumptions: Safety Services is the correct implementation module for DP5 (schema-driven feature definition, validation, and constraint enforcement) with containment score 0.92 and minimal external coupling.
Multi-modal fusion (DP1) can be owned by Safety Services or integrated via a strict Telemetry interface contract without architectural compromise.
Operational policies and worksite safety plans can be formalized as schema constraints and enforced via Safety Services without requiring external policy engines.
High-frequency Telemetry observations can be validated asynchronously without blocking ingestion or violating safety-critical latency requirements.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can be constrained to emit only schema-compliant, canonically-formatted observations via interface contracts.
A single authoritative schema-compliant repository in Safety Services is feasible and does not create unacceptable query latency for planning/execution functions.
open_risks: DP1 ownership ambiguity: If multi-modal fusion remains distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation without clear ownership or interface contract, schema validation will receive heterogeneous observations, increasing validation complexity and reducing model integrity. Mitigation: Explicitly assign DP1 ownership or enforce strict Telemetry interface contract.

Schema evolution lag: If operational policy updates occur outside Safety Services, schema constraints may not reflect current safety intent, creating consistency gaps. Mitigation: Designate Safety Services as authoritative schema owner and enforce policy-to-schema update workflows via Tasking interface.

Cached replica consistency: If Vehicle or Teleoperation cache schema-compliant features locally, constraint enforcement in Safety Services may not detect violations in stale data. Mitigation: Enforce single-source-of-truth policy; permit read-only caching but require authoritative constraint checks via Safety Services.

Ingestion pipeline throughput bottleneck: Synchronous schema validation may block high-frequency Telemetry observations, violating latency requirements. Mitigation: Design asynchronous validation with buffering; use SafetyAlert for non-blocking failure communication.

Safety-critical latency: Query-time constraint enforcement in Safety Services must not introduce unacceptable latency for planning/execution functions. Mitigation: Define and verify latency acceptance criteria; optimize query performance and caching strategies.
evidence_needed: Explicit ownership assignment or interface contract for DP1 (multi-modal fusion): document whether fusion is co-located in Safety Services or distributed with a canonical format specification and validation rules.
Formal Tasking interface contract: specify how operational policy updates trigger schema validation and evolution workflows in Safety Services.
Single-source-of-truth policy document: define authoritative repository ownership, caching rules, and constraint enforcement procedures.
Safety Services ingestion pipeline design: document asynchronous validation architecture, buffering strategy, and SafetyAlert communication protocol.
Acceptance criteria for DP1–DP5 integration: quantified success rates (≥99% validation success), latency thresholds, and percentile targets.

Verification plan: test cases confirming schema definition aligns with worksite safety plan, raw observations are successfully ingested and normalized, repository queries return schema-compliant features, and SafetyAlert correctly flags violations.
Latency analysis: measure query-time constraint enforcement latency and confirm it meets planning/execution function requirements.
